title: SEGMENT 15 — Why drugs stop working: resistance and clonal evolution

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Why drugs stop working: resistance and clonal evolution
If you treat a cancer patient with a brilliant targeted drug like imatinib, you can get a beautiful response.
Tumor markers drop.
The patient feels better.
Scans look great.
And then, sometimes months later, sometimes years later, the cancer comes back.
And when it comes back, the drug doesn't work anymore.
Why?
The answer is one of the deepest ideas in cancer biology, and it's why this whole field has, in the last twenty years, started calling itself "evolutionary oncology.
" Cancer evolves.
Inside the patient.
In response to the drug.

Think of a tumor not as a uniform mass of identical cells but as a population — a huge population, billions of cells, all descended from one original renegade but having accumulated different additional mutations along the way.
Most are basically alike, but here and there a cell has acquired some random extra mutation that makes it slightly different.
In an untreated tumor, those variants don't particularly matter — they're a minority, lost in the crowd.
Now introduce a drug.
The drug kills the cells that are sensitive to it — the vast majority.
But somewhere in the population, by sheer chance, there might be a cell with a mutation that happens to make the drug ineffective.
Before the drug, that cell was just one of billions, no advantage to it.
After the drug, suddenly it's one of the only cells left alive, and it has the whole tumor's worth of space and resources to expand into.
It grows.
Its descendants — all carrying the resistance mutation — re-fill the tumor.
The cancer returns, but now made entirely of resistant cells.
The drug is useless.
This is Darwinian evolution playing out on a timescale of months, in real time, inside a single patient.
The drug is the selection pressure.
The cancer is the population.
Resistance is the adaptive response.
Let me give you a few concrete flavors of resistance, because they recur across many drugs.
On-target resistance.
The cancer acquires a mutation in the very protein the drug is hitting, in a way that prevents the drug from binding while still letting the protein do its bad job.
The classic example: imatinib's target, BCR-ABL, can acquire a mutation called T315I in its kinase pocket.
The mutation swaps a single amino acid for one with a bulky side chain that physically blocks imatinib from getting into the pocket.
The kinase keeps firing; the drug bounces off.
The same pattern happens with EGFR inhibitors in lung cancer — patients respond beautifully to first-generation drugs, then relapse with a mutation called T790M in EGFR that prevents binding.
We then have second and third-generation drugs designed to overcome those mutations.
The cancer mutates again.
We design fourth-generation.
It's a chess game.
Bypass resistance.
The cancer leaves the target alone but turns on a parallel pathway that produces the same growth signal.
The drug is hitting the right thing; the cell just routes around it.
A lung cancer treated with an EGFR inhibitor can amplify a related receptor called MET, which feeds into the same downstream signaling without needing EGFR.
Lineage plasticity.
This one is genuinely strange.
Some cancers, under drug pressure, transform into a different kind of cancer entirely.
A lung adenocarcinoma treated with an EGFR inhibitor can transform into small-cell lung cancer — a histologically completely different disease — which no longer depends on EGFR at all.
Prostate cancers under hormone-blocking therapy can transform into a neuroendocrine cancer that no longer needs the androgen receptor.
The cell drops the original identity that the drug was exploiting and reinvents itself.
Microenvironmental resistance.
The tumor neighborhood gets reorganized to support survival despite the drug.
The fibroblasts make more protective matrix.
The vasculature thickens.
The immune environment becomes more suppressive.
Even cancer cells that should be sensitive to the drug survive because the neighborhood is sheltering them.
The conceptual lesson: every cancer therapy creates a selection pressure, and any population large enough — and a tumor is always large enough — will evolve to escape the pressure if it can.
The arms race we discussed with antibiotics is exactly what's going on inside an oncology patient, just on a different timescale.
This shapes how thoughtful oncologists deploy drugs.
They combine drugs, hoping the cancer can't simultaneously develop resistance to two or three at once.
They time treatments to apply pressure without giving the cancer breathing room.

There's an experimental strategy called adaptive therapy, championed by a researcher named Robert Gatenby, where you deliberately dose intermittently so that drug-sensitive cells aren't entirely eliminated — keeping them around, paradoxically, to outcompete resistant cells when the drug is off.
It's an explicit application of ecological reasoning to cancer treatment.
We treat the cancer like an invasive species rather than like an opponent to be annihilated.
The mature view: cure is sometimes possible, especially early.
Long-term control, with the cancer evolving and us evolving our drugs in response, is increasingly the realistic goal for many advanced cancers.
Cancer is becoming, for some patients, a chronic disease — which is itself a triumph compared to where we were forty years ago.
